note_data.title: Kinematics
keyPoints: **Displacement** is the distance moved in a specified direction from a fixed point
**Velocity** is the rate of change of displacement with respect to time
**Acceleration** is the rate of change of velocity with respect to time
Use **v² = u² + 2as** when time is not given or required in the problem
Draw a **velocity-time graph** to visualize motion and calculate displacement using area under curve
For **projectile motion**, treat horizontal and vertical components separately using appropriate equations
When object reaches maximum height in projectile motion, **vertical velocity = 0**
**Gradient of displacement-time graph** gives velocity at that instant
**Gradient of velocity-time graph** gives acceleration at that instant

definitions.definition: The distance moved in a specified direction from a fixed point
The rate of change of displacement with respect to time
The rate of change of velocity with respect to time
Motion of an object under the influence of gravity alone

howToAnswer.instruction: Give one fact in one sentence. No explanation needed.
Show all working clearly. State the formula, substitute values, give final answer with units.
Use appropriate kinematic equations and show complete method
Draw accurate graph with labeled axes and key values marked

modelAnswer.question: A cricket ball is thrown vertically upward from ground level in Lahore with initial velocity 20 m/s. Taking g = 10 m/s², find the maximum height reached.
modelAnswer.answer: At maximum height, v = 0. Using v² = u² + 2as where u = 20 m/s, v = 0, a = -10 m/s². 0² = 20² + 2(-10)s. 0 = 400 - 20s. s = 20 m. Maximum height = 20 m.
markBreakdown: Mark 1: Correct identification that v = 0 at maximum height
Mark 2: Correct substitution into v² = u² + 2as with negative acceleration
Mark 3: Correct calculation giving s = 20 m

commonMistakes: Never confuse distance with displacement — displacement has direction, distance does not
Students often forget to use negative acceleration when object moves against gravity
Always check if acceleration is constant before using kinematic equations
Never use average velocity formula when acceleration is not zero
Students often confuse speed with velocity — velocity includes direction
examinerTips: This topic appears in every Paper 2 examination
Always draw a diagram showing directions and label all given information
Check units are consistent throughout your calculation
For projectile motion questions, always separate into horizontal and vertical components
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: Velocity-time graph showing straight line for constant acceleration, with area under curve representing displacement and gradient representing acceleration
pastPaperRefs: This topic was tested in M/J 2023 P2 Q3 (6 marks) — they asked students to find when velocity is zero given displacement s = (t+2)(t-5)², requiring differentiation and solving quadratic equations
This topic appeared in O/N 2022 P1 Q4 (4 marks) — candidates had to find displacement expression from velocity formula and calculate distance travelled over specific time interval
This topic was examined in M/J 2024 P2 Q2 (5 marks) — students needed to find exact speed at t=1 given displacement s = t³ + (1/3)t² - 1, testing differentiation skills
This topic featured in O/N 2023 P1 Q5 (4 marks) — question required finding displacement expression (9/4)sin(t/3) from given velocity conditions

workedExample.question: A particle moves such that its displacement from point O is given by s = (t+2)(t-5)². Find the time when the velocity is zero. [4 marks]
steps: Step 1: Find velocity by differentiating displacement. v = ds/dt = d/dt[(t+2)(t-5)²]
Step 4: Set v = 0: (t-5)(3t-1) = 0, so t = 5 or t = 1/3
workedExample.answer: The velocity is zero when t = 1/3 seconds and t = 5 seconds
workedExample.markScheme: M1 for correct differentiation using product rule, A1 for simplified velocity expression, M1 for setting v=0, A1 for both correct time values

examinerWarnings: Examiner Report 2023: 'Many candidates confused distance and displacement' — always check if the question asks for total distance (always positive) or displacement (can be negative)
Examiner Report 2024: 'Students frequently forgot to use absolute values when calculating distance' — when finding distance travelled, integrate |v| not just v
Examiner Report 2022: 'Common error was incorrect application of product rule in differentiation' — always expand (t+a)(t+b)² correctly before differentiating or use product rule systematically
note_data.keyDiagram: Students must be able to draw a displacement-time graph showing: positive and negative displacement regions, turning points where velocity equals zero, and be able to sketch the corresponding velocity-time graph below it. The area under the velocity-time curve represents displacement change, while the absolute area gives distance travelled. Mark clearly the axes (s vs t and v vs t), zero lines, and any given time intervals.
note_data.quickRecap: Velocity = ds/dt, acceleration = dv/dt = d²s/dt². When v = 0, particle changes direction. Distance travelled requires integrating |v|, while displacement change integrates v directly. Always differentiate displacement to get velocity, then set equal to zero to find turning points.
